name: Abu Simbel
oneLiner: UNESCO World Heritage Site in southern Egypt

bio.extract: Abu Simbel is a historic site comprising two massive rock-cut temples in the village of Abu Simbel, Aswan Governorate, Upper Egypt, near the border with Sudan. It is located on the western bank of Lake Nasser, about 230 km (140 mi) southwest of Aswan. Its latitude of 22° 20′ 13″ N is 1.0978°, which are 122 km, south of the tropic of Cancer.

paintings.title: Frieze inside the Great Temple of Abu Simbel

story.extract: Frieze inside the Great Temple of Abu Simbel. Abu Simbel is a historic site comprising two massive rock-cut temples in the village of Abu Simbel, Aswan Governorate, Upper Egypt, near the border with Sudan. It is located on the western bank of Lake Nasser, about 230 km (140 mi) southwest of Aswan.

facts.text: The complex consists of two temples. The larger one is dedicated to Ra-Horakhty, Ptah and Amun, Egypt's three state deities of the time, and features four large statues of Ramesses II in the facade.

During his reign, Ramesses II embarked on an extensive building program throughout Egypt and Nubia, which Egypt controlled. As a major source of gold and many other precious trade goods, Nubia was of great importance to the Egyptians.

With the passage of time, the temples fell into disuse and the Great Temple eventually became mostly covered by a sand dune. By the 6th century BC, the sand already covered the statues of the main temple up to their knees.

paintings.title: Photograph of the Great Temple by Francis Frith in 1857

story.extract: Aerial view of the hill covering the rear of the Abu Simbel temples in 1969. story.extract: Genevese architect Jean Jacquet, a UNESCO expert, makes an architectural survey of the Great Temple of Ramesses II, in order to determine where to cut the temple in pieces.
